comment_text.sn12.26:1.2: Upavāṇa was nursing the Buddha at SN 7.13:1.3.
comment_text.sn12.26:3.1: Like the previous, this discourse only hints at the twelve links.

root_text.sn12.26:1.2: Atha kho āyasmā upavāṇo yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā upavāṇo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn12.26:2.1: “Santi pana, bhante, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā sayaṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.26:2.2: Santi pana, bhante, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā paraṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.26:2.3: Santi pana, bhante, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.26:2.4: Santi pana, bhante, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.26:2.5: Idha no, bhante, bhagavā kiṁvādī kimakkhāyī kathaṁ byākaramānā ca mayaṁ vuttavādino ceva bhagavato assāma, na ca bhagavantaṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyyāma, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyyāma, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānupāto gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyyā”ti?
root_text.sn12.26:3.4: Iti vadaṁ vuttavādī ceva me assa, na ca maṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyya, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyya, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānupāto gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyya.
root_text.sn12.26:4.1: Tatra, upavāṇa, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā sayaṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti, tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.26:4.4: yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.26:5.1: Tatra, upavāṇa, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā sayaṅkataṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn12.26:5.4: yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ dukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjatī”ti.

translation_text.sn12.26:1.2: Then Venerable Upavāṇa went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him:
translation_text.sn12.26:2.1: “Sir, there are some ascetics and brahmins who declare that suffering is made by oneself.
translation_text.sn12.26:2.2: There are some who declare that suffering is made by another.
translation_text.sn12.26:2.3: There are some who declare that suffering is made by both oneself and another.
translation_text.sn12.26:2.4: There are some who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another.
translation_text.sn12.26:2.5: What does the Buddha say about this? How does he explain it? How should we answer so as to repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth? How should we explain in line with his teaching, with no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism?”
translation_text.sn12.26:3.1: “Upavāṇa, I have said that suffering is dependently originated.
translation_text.sn12.26:3.4: In speaking like this, you would repeat what I have said, and not misrepresent me with an untruth. You would explain in line with my teaching, and there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism.
translation_text.sn12.26:4.1: In the case of those ascetics and brahmins who declare that suffering is made by oneself, that’s dependent on contact. …
translation_text.sn12.26:4.4: In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, that’s also dependent on contact.
translation_text.sn12.26:5.1: In the case of those ascetics and brahmins who declare that suffering is made by oneself, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. …
translation_text.sn12.26:5.4: In the case of those who declare that suffering arises anomalously, not made by oneself or another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.”
